title: Writing Expressions
description: Turn words into algebra expressions without losing what the words mean.

scaffolded.title: Turn words into algebra
scaffolded.body: Word problems are just math in disguise. Name the unknown number **x**, then translate the key words into symbols. Tap through the mini-dictionary.

steps.text: First, whenever a problem says "a number," you can call it x.
"plus" or "more than" means add (+). "minus" or "less than" means subtract (−).
"times" means multiply, written as a number in front (like 3x). Translate piece by piece and any phrase becomes an expression. Your turn!

scaffolded.prompt: "A number plus 5" can be written as...
scaffolded.hint: Call the unknown number x. Then "plus 5" tells you which operation to tack on.

feedback.correct: Yes! Call the number x, and "plus" adds 5, that's x + 5.
incorrect: Start with x for the unknown number.

explanation.title: Name the unknown
explanation.body: Translation has two parts: name the unknown with a letter, then turn each key word into its operation.

scaffolded.prompt: "Three times a number" can be written as...
scaffolded.hint: "Three times" means three copies of the number. How do we write copies in a compact way?

feedback.correct: Exactly, "three times" means three copies, written 3x.
incorrect: When you multiply, the number sits in front of the variable.

explanation.title: Times means copies
explanation.body: "Times" is multiplication, and multiplying a variable is written compactly with the count in front.

scaffolded.prompt: A story says "a number plus 4 equals 10." On the scale, x plus 4 balances 10. Change the scale to find the number, keep both pans balanced!

followUpMc.prompt: What number made the story true?

feedback.correct: Yes! Translate, balance, solve, the number is 6.
incorrect: x is alone now. Read the remaining weight.

scaffolded.hint: Translate the words into x + 4 = 10, then undo +4.

feedback.correct: Nice, the story became x + 4 = 10, and x = 6.
incorrect: Keep the scale balanced by changing both sides.

explanation.title: Words to equation
explanation.body: Once words become an equation, solving is the same balance move as before: undo the operation on both sides.

scaffolded.title: Build it in pieces
scaffolded.body: Longer phrases have more than one part. Build the expression one piece at a time, in the order the words give you. Tap through an example.

steps.text: Take "four more than twice a number." Start with the main part: "twice a number" is 2x.
Then "four more" means add 4 onto the end.
Snap the pieces together in order: 2x + 4. Build yours the same way, main part first, then the extra. Your turn!

scaffolded.prompt: Build the expression for: "two more than three times a number."
scaffolded.hint: First write three times a number. Then add two more.

feedback.correct: Exactly! Main part first (3x), then "two more" adds 2 → 3x + 2. Built it perfectly.
incorrect: Build it in order: three times a number, then two more.
Translate in pieces: the times-a-number part first, then add the two more.

explanation.title: Build expressions in pieces
explanation.body: Translate a phrase one piece at a time and assemble the parts in the order the words give them.

incorrect: Use x for the number, then subtract 4.
Two times x is 2x. Then add 1.

mastery.prompt: Which expression means "5 more than a number"?

incorrect: More than means add 5 to the number.
